raw_response:
{
  "communicative_function": "Endorsing and transmitting a hierarchical moral judgment on types of wrongdoing by citing Theophrastus as philosophical authority",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "Sins (or moral faults) admit of comparative degrees of gravity",
    "The distinction between lust-driven and anger-driven faults is philosophically valid rather than merely conventional",
    "Lust-driven faults are morally worse than anger-driven faults",
    "Theophrastus' judgment on this point is sound and worthy of a philosopher"
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "Internally coherent: the parenthetical concession acknowledges the vulgarity of ranking sins yet preserves the legitimacy of the specific ranking offered",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": "The audience accepts a eudaimonistic or Stoic-Peripatetic framework in which emotions can be ranked by moral fault, and the claim is used for ethical self-examination rather than legal or social punishment",
  "conditions_for_misleading": "The statement is treated as a universal or divinely sanctioned ranking independent of ancient ethical presuppositions, or is applied to justify differential punishment without further argument"
}
